Title: Access control
Description: Rules to control physical and logical access to information and other associated assets shall be established and implemented based on business and information security requirements.

Title: Access granting process used
Description: Implement a formal access granting process that ensures new access privileges are assigned based on the principle of least privilege, and require at least one employee to endorse the granting of new access.
Instructions: #### Action ItemsIf you already have an access granting process in place, make sure to document it. Review it on a regular basis.If you don't have an access granting process now, create a simple process that works for your organization, and document that policy in a place that is easy for employees to view.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Documentation of the access granting process and evidence of the system used to manage access requests (e.g., request form, dedicated Slack channel). The process must clearly specify who is authorized to approve access requests, unless this is already defined elsewhere such as the access management policy.
Frameworks: cfr_21_part_11_v1, cis_ig1_v1, eu_dora_v1, gdpr_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800-53_v5, nist_800_171_v1, oneleet_pro_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1, soc2_v1

Title: Access management policy established
Description: Systematic controls are established in the access management policy for managing user access rights, ensuring appropriate, authorized access to systems and data.
Instructions: ### Action itemsDocument an access management policy covering user lifecycle from onboarding through role changes and termination. Define processes for access requests, approvals, provisioning, and periodic reviews. Establish role-based access control framework with principle of least privilege. Create procedures for managing privileged access rights, emergency access, and service accounts.Document requirements for access monitoring, automatic revocation triggers, and regular access reviews.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Access management policy uploaded to the Oneleet policy builder.2. PDF upload of the access management policy with a dated version history.
Frameworks: cfr_21_part_11_v1, eu_dora_v1, gdpr_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_direct_v1, iso27001_v1, iso42001_v1, nist_800-53_v5, nist_800_171_v1, oneleet_pro_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1, soc2_v1

Title: Access requests to sensitive data required
Description: To access sensitive data, employees must submit access requests that are approved and monitored.
Instructions: ### Action itemsEstablish a formal access request process for requesting access to sensitive data, include justification for access, duration of access needed, and level of access required. Implement a standardized request form or ticketing system. ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Access Management policy uploaded to the Oneleet policy builder.2. A screenshot of your access approval system(e.g., Jira, ServiceNow) that contains the resource requested, the requester and approver, the justification for access, and the expiration/duration of the access. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.
Frameworks: ccpa_v1, cis_ig1_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800-53_v5, nist_800_171_v1

Title: Access requests to sensitive infrastructure required
Description: To access sensitive infrastructure components, employees must submit access requests that are approved and monitored. This control helps ensure that only authorized personnel can access critical infrastructure, minimizing potential risks.
Instructions: ### Action items### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Access Management policy uploaded to the Oneleet policy builder.2. A screenshot of your access approval system (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow) that contains the resource requested, the requester and approver, the justification for access, and the expiration/duration of the access. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.
Frameworks: hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800-53_v5, nist_800_171_v1

Title: Access revoking process enforced
Description: Implement a regular access review process to promptly remove access privileges from employees who no longer require them. This ensures that former employees or users do not retain unauthorized access to organizational resources.
Instructions: ### Action itemsFirst, ensure that you have a process documented that outlines how you revoke access that is no longer needed. This can be as simple as an access review session each quarter where your organization does an audit on all systems and ensures that no one is over or under provisioned### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Access Management policy uploaded to the Oneleet policy builder.2. A screenshot of your access revoking procedure detailing the steps taken when a user is removed.3. A screenshot showing a recurring calendar invite to review user access. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.4. A dated log of previous access revocations.
Frameworks: cis_ig1_v1, eu_dora_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800-53_v5, nist_800_171_v1

Title: Access rights
Description: Access rights to information and other associated assets shall be provisioned, reviewed, modified and removed in accordance with the organization's topic-specific policy on and rules for access control.

Title: Access to source code
Description: Read and write access to source code, development tools and software libraries shall be appropriately managed.

Title: Account inventory maintained
Description: An inventory of user accounts on critical and high-risk vendors that have access to in-scope systems and services is maintained, including essential details such as account owners, access privileges, associated roles, and vendor relationships where applicable. The inventory is reviewed and updated at least annually.
Instructions: ### Action itemsAn inventory in this control refers to a singular point of truth about all of the accounts in your product and your third party vendors. This could be a Notion table, a Google Sheets file, or a SAAS product that manages your accounts for you. The primary two facets of this control are that 1) **all** user accounts are listed as well as their access level and 2) this inventory is reviewed regularly and kept up to date.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet access review completed for all critical vendors.2. Account inventory listing all accounts on third party vendors and all accounts in your product, including the date the inventory was last reviewed.
Frameworks: cis_ig1_v1, eu_dora_v1, gdpr_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800-53_v5, nist_800_171_v1, oneleet_pro_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1, soc2_v1

Title: Audit log access restricted
Description: Ensure audit information and audit logging tools are safeguarded against unauthorized access, modification, and deletion. In order to maintain the integrity and reliability of audit information, access to audit logging functionality must be restricted to a limited subset of privileged users or roles that have an explicit business need.
Instructions: ### Action Items- Limit access to audit logging tools to a subset of authorized personnel with defined roles.- Implement access controls to prevent unauthorized access, modification, or deletion of audit records, audit log settings, and audit reports.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Audit log management policy uploaded to the Oneleet policy builder.2. Logs showing all users with access to audit logs, their access levels, and the business need for the elevated access.3. Screenshots from your logging system's IAM/Permissions page(e.g., AWS IAM, Datadog User Roles) showing that access to the audit logs is granted only to specific users/roles. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.

Title: Authentication information
Description: Allocation and management of authentication information shall be controlled by a management process, including advising personnel on the appropriate handling of authentication information.

Title: Authenticators initially and periodically tested
Description: Initially and periodically test devices (tokens/cards) that bear or generate identification code/password information to ensure proper function and no unauthorized alteration.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Define a test plan and schedule for authenticators (upon receipt and at defined intervals).2. Test for tampering, malfunction, and firmware integrity; document results.3. Replace failed/altered devices and investigate anomalies.4. Maintain test records and link to asset inventory.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Authenticator test logs and certificates of conformance.- Firmware integrity check results.- Asset inventory referencing device test dates/results.- CAPA records for failed devices.

Title: Automated decision-making and profiling has oversight
Description: This requirements establishes safeguards for automated decision-making processes, including profiling, to align with GDPR requirements and protect data subjects' rights.
Instructions: ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Inventory of automated decision-making processes, including their legal basis and implemented safeguards- Consent forms for automated decision-making, clearly explaining the process and its implications- Documented procedure for handling requests for human intervention, expression of views, and contesting of decisions**Action items**If you identified automated decision-making or profiling processes in your risk assessment for high-impact data processing, this control is applicable.For each of these processes, determine if it falls under permitted exceptions: contractual necessity, legal authorization, or explicit consent.If not a permitted exception, implement one of the following measures:1. Remove the automated decision-making process and incorporate human intervention2. Obtain explicit consent from data subjects for the automated decision-making3. Modify the process to fall under contractual necessity or seek legal authorizationFor all automated decision-making processes, regardless of exception:1. Implement mechanisms for human intervention2. Establish a process for data subjects to express their views3. Create a procedure for data subjects to contest decisionsDocument all automated decision-making processes and their compliance measures.

Title: Automatic user logoff enforced
Description: Implement an automatic logoff mechanism to terminate user sessions after a period of inactivity.
Instructions: Examples of evidence that fulfills this control1. Access Management policy uploaded to the Oneleet policy builder.2. A screenshot or excerpt from your policy that requires automatic user logoff and defines the max idle time allowed.3. A screenshot or excerpt from your system showing that the max idle time defined in your policy is enforced.4. A screenshot of logs or an activity report(e.g., CloudWatch Logs, Datadog) showing an inactive session timeout/logoff event.
Frameworks: hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, nist_800-53_v5

Title: Automatic user session termination enforced
Description: User sessions must be automatically terminated all defined events outlined in the Compliance NIST 800-171 Policy. Conditions may include inactivity for a specified period (e.g., 15 minutes), time-of-day restrictions on access, and responses to suspicious activity or security incidents. Automatic session termination ensures system security by ending processes associated with the user's logical session.
Instructions: ### Action Items- Configure automatic session termination settings as defined in the Access Management Policy to any web applications and databases where CUI is accessible.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Screenshots showing configurations demonstrating sessions are automatically terminated in systems where users can access CUI.- A screenshot showing a log of automatically terminated sessions.

Title: Biometric signature uniqueness ensured
Description: Design biometric‑based signatures so they cannot be used by anyone other than the genuine owner.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Use biometric tech with liveness detection and template protection.2. Validate FAR/FRR performance and anti‑spoofing.3. Encrypt biometric templates; restrict access; document data retention/deletion.4. Provide non‑biometric fallback with equivalent controls when biometrics unavailable, with additional verification.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Vendor documentation on liveness/anti‑spoofing and performance.- Validation/acceptance test results for biometric devices.- Access controls and encryption settings for biometric templates.- SOPs for biometric enrollment, use, and fallback.

Title: Changes to PHI logged
Description: Track all changes made to PHI, including additions, modifications, and deletions, to ensure data integrity and enable audit trails.
Instructions: Examples of evidence that fulfills this control:- Logs documenting changes to PHI, including before-and-after states if feasible.- Procedures for reviewing and auditing PHI change logs.
Frameworks: hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, nist_800-53_v5

Title: Credential loss response executed
Description: Follow loss‑management procedures to deauthorize lost/stolen/missing tokens or devices and issue replacements under rigorous controls.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Provide 24/7 process to report lost tokens/cards.2. Immediately revoke compromised authenticators and force credential reset.3. Bind replacement issuance to positive identity verification and ticketed approval.4. Maintain inventory of authenticators and chain of custody.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Incident tickets for lost/stolen device reports and revocations.- Authenticator inventory with assignment history.- Replacement issuance logs with ID verification.- SIEM logs showing revocation events.

Title: Dedicated administrator accounts used
Description: Restrict administrator privileges to dedicated administrator accounts on enterprise assets. Conduct general computing activities, such as internet browsing, email, and productivity suite use, from the user’s primary, non-privileged account.
Instructions: ### Action itemsEnsure all employees are using non-privileged accounts for their day-to-day tasks and that administrative duties are performed from separate accounts with the necessary privileges. Regular audits should be conducted to ensure adherence to this policy.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet monitors passing for supported integrations.2. A screenshot of the User List or IAM dashboard showing privilege levels and a clear distinction between admin and non-admin accounts.

Title: Default accounts disabled
Description: Disable default accounts on enterprise assets and software, such as root, administrator, and other pre-configured vendor accounts, where possible. Example implementations can include: disabling default accounts or making them unusable.
Instructions: ### Action itemsReview all enterprise assets and software to identify any default accounts. Once identified, either disable these accounts or change their credentials to make them unusable. Ensure to document this process and keep it up-to-date for future reference and audits.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet monitors passing for supported integrations.2. A screenshot of your default account management settings.3. A link or screenshot of your process documentation for disabling default accounts.4. A report or log showing the disabled or modified default accounts.

Title: Dormant accounts disabled
Description: Monitor for dormant user accounts and disable or remove accounts that have been inactive for an extended period.
Instructions: ### Action itemsEstablish a policy for reviewing user accounts on a regular basis. Accounts that are no longer being used should be deactivated or removed entirely. Use an automated system for tracking inactivity if possible, and manually follow up on any accounts that haven't had any activity for an extended period.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet access review completed for all critical vendors and Oneleet monitors passing for all supported integrations.2. Documented account review process, and screenshots of automated system for tracking inactivity.
Frameworks: cis_ig1_v1, nist_800_171_v1, oneleet_essentials_v1, oneleet_pro_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1, soc2_v1

Title: Electronic signature components managed
Description: Require multi‑factor or multi-component e‑signature mechanisms for non‑biometric systems.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Implement dual components (ID + password).2. Enforce full entry for first signing.3. Apply least component for subsequent signings.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Configuration showing multi‑component signature setup.- Logs demonstrating first full entry and subsequent shortcuts.- Policy describing component usage.

Title: Emergency access procedure established
Description: Establish and maintain a procedure for granting authorized personnel access to critical information during emergencies.
Instructions: **Examples of evidence that fulfills this control**- A documented emergency access procedure outlining the steps for granting access during emergencies.- Logs or records of emergency access events, including personnel involved, date, time, and reason for access.- Evidence of testing or simulations showing how the procedure works during an emergency scenario.- Records of actual emergency situations demonstrating adherence to the procedure.
Frameworks: hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1

Title: Employee access regularly reviewed
Description: Employee access is reviewed at least annually to ensure that access privileges are appropriate and that former employees or users do not retain unauthorized access.
Instructions: ### Action itemsFirst, establish that you have a process documented that outlines how you revoke access that is no longer needed. This can be as simple as an access review session each quarter where your organization does an audit on all systems and ensures that no one is over or under provisioned. Document the actions taken during these sessions.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet access review completed for all critical vendors2. Documented access review process outlining review frequency, what systems are reviewed, and procedures for revoking unnecessary access.
Frameworks: cfr_21_part_11_v1, eu_dora_v1, gdpr_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800_171_v1, oneleet_pro_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1, soc2_v1

Title: IAM Inventory Maintained
Description: Maintain a centralized inventory of all individuals, groups, roles, services, and devices with access to organizational systems.
Instructions: ### Action itemsMaintain a single source of truth for all identifiers using an IAM tool, cloud-native service, or an organized database.- Assign unique identifiers to individuals, groups, roles, services, and devices using a consistent naming convention (e.g., "EMP-<name>" for employees, "CON-<name>" for contractors).- Add labels to identifiers indicating the entity's role or status, such as "Contractor," "Full-time Employee," or "External User.”- The reuse of identifiers is never permitted unless an explicit period of time before reuse is otherwise established and defined in your access management policy.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Screenshot or link to your inventory or IAM tools (e.g., Okta, Azure AD) showing unique identifiers for all individuals, groups, roles, services, and devices.

Title: Identity management
Description: The full life cycle of identities shall be managed.

Title: Information access restriction
Description: Access to information and other associated assets shall be restricted in accordance with the established topic-specific policy on access control.

Title: Key inventory maintained
Description: Maintain an inventory of all shared or production access tokens to ensure proper management, tracking, and security of our key assets.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsEstablish a centralized key management system or database. Create a standardized process for recording new keys, including all required information (creator, service description, creation date, rotation date, current owner).Implement a regular review process to establish that the inventory is up-to-date. Set up automated alerts for key rotation based on predefined schedules or risk assessments.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Screenshots of a centralized key management system (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager) containing all required information for each key. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.2. Key management logs showing dates of generation, rotation, and revocation.3. Documentation of regular key inventory reviews, including dates and responsible personnel.

Title: MFA required for administrative access
Description: Require multi-factor authentication (MFA) for administrative access to critical systems and services. MFA mitigates the risk of unauthorized administrative access and helps protect sensitive administrative functions.
Instructions: ### Action itemsEnsure that MFA is enabled for all administrative accounts on your critical systems and services. This can be done in the settings of most systems and services, or through the use of third-party MFA applications.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet monitors passing for supported integrations, with MFA enforced across all critical services and infrastructure.2. Screenshots of your MFA settings across all critical services and infrastructure. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.
Frameworks: cis_ig1_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800_171_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1

Title: MFA required for applications
Description: Require all externally-exposed enterprise or third-party applications to enforce MFA, where supported. Enforcing MFA through a directory service or SSO provider is a satisfactory implementation of this safeguard.
Instructions: ### Action itemsImplement MFA on all your externally-exposed enterprise or third-party applications. If the application does not support MFA, consider using a directory service or Single Sign-On (SSO) provider that does.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet monitors passing for supported integrations, with MFA enforced across all critical services and infrastructure.2. Screenshots of your MFA settings across all critical services and infrastructure. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.3. Screenshots or links to your MFA policy documentation or user agreements.
Frameworks: cis_ig1_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, nist_800_171_v1, oneleet_essentials_v1, oneleet_pro_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1

Title: MFA required for critical services
Description: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is required for accessing critical services and infrastructure unless a documented exception is in place where MFA is not supported.
Instructions: ### Action itemsImplement MFA across all your systems, especially those that contain sensitive data. Ensure that your team understands the importance of MFA and how to use it. Training sessions may be necessary to facilitate a smooth transition to regular use of MFA, and this policy should be clearly documented in an accessible location for all employees.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet monitors passing for supported integrations, with MFA enforced across all critical services and infrastructure.2. Screenshots of your MFA settings across all critical services and infrastructure. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.
Frameworks: cfr_21_part_11_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, nist_800_171_v1, oneleet_essentials_v1, oneleet_pro_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1, soc2_v1

Title: MFA required for infrastructure access
Description: Require multi-factor authentication (MFA) for accessing sensitive infrastructure. MFA adds an extra layer of security by requiring users to provide additional authentication factors beyond their passwords.
Instructions: ### Action itemsImplement MFA across all your systems, especially those that contain sensitive data. Ensure that your team understands the importance of MFA and how to use it. Training sessions may be necessary to facilitate a smooth transition to regular use of MFA, and this policy should be clearly documented in an accessible location for all employees.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet monitors passing for supported integrations, with MFA enforced across all critical services and infrastructure.2. Screenshots of your MFA settings across all critical services and infrastructure. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.3. Screenshots or links to your MFA policy documentation or user agreements.4. Screenshots of MFA compliance reports from your identity provider.
Frameworks: eu_dora_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800_171_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1

Title: MFA required for network access
Description: Require multi-factor authentication (MFA) for accessing the organization's network. MFA strengthens network security by requiring users to provide additional authentication factors during login attempts.
Instructions: ### Action itemsEnsure that MFA is enabled for all of your organization's accounts. This could be achieved through various means such as text messages, phone calls, or authenticator apps.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Screenshot of your MFA settings in your organization's network access control.- Screenshots or links to your MFA policy documentation or user agreements.- Screenshots of MFA compliance reports from your identity provider.

Title: Maintenance control process established
Description: Establish a process to approve and monitor maintenance and diagnostic activities requiring remote network access to manage or maintain systems—this encompasses all maintenance activities for applications hosted on a cloud provider.
Instructions: ### Action itemsRequire approval for all maintenance activities, such as software updates, diagnostics, or troubleshooting performed remotely. Use a centralized location (e.g., Slack, ticketing tools like Jira or Zendesk, or email) to log and track approvals for all maintenance activities. Leverage built-in cloud monitoring tools (e.g., AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor, GCP Logging) to track maintenance sessions. Configure automatic session and network connection termination for remote maintenance activities.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- A link or screenshot of past maintenance requests.- Configuration settings or screenshots demonstrating automated termination of remote sessions after maintenance is complete.

Title: Maintenance personnel authorization process established
Description: Establish a process for authorizing maintenance personnel and organizations to ensure only qualified and authorized individuals perform maintenance on the system. Verify access authorizations for non-escorted personnel and designate authorized personnel to supervise maintenance activities when required.
Instructions: ### Action items- Develop and document a process for authorizing maintenance personnel. Ensure to outline how individuals will receive required access such as issuing temporary credentials.- Maintain a current list of authorized maintenance organizations or personnel, including their roles and authorizations.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- A link or screenshot of current authorized maintenance organizations or personnel.- A link or screenshot of your documented maintenance personnel authorization process.

Title: Maintenance tools managed and secured
Description: Implement a process to approve, control, and monitor the use of system maintenance tools, ensuring they are free from malicious code and do not compromise the security of CUI. Additionally, prevent unauthorized removal of system maintenance equipment containing CUI by sanitizing, retaining, or securely destroying such equipment as outlined in the Asset Management Policy.
Instructions: ### Action items- Establish a formal approval process for adding new tools to the inventory.- Regularly review and remove outdated or unsupported tools from use.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Screenshot or link to asset management inventory including approved system maintenance tools.- Screenshot or link to your document outlining the process for adding new maintenance tools to the inventory.

Title: Password expiration and renewal enforced
Description: Periodically check, recall, or revise identification codes and passwords (e.g., password aging) to maintain security and integrity.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Enforce password rotation or equivalent risk‑based controls (e.g., longer passphrases + breach‑monitoring) per policy.2. Periodically re‑issue/re‑validate credentials for signers.3. Disable dormant accounts and revoke credentials upon role change/termination.4. Document the cadence and results of these checks.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Password policy showing aging/rotation or compensating controls.- Reports of periodic credential reviews and re‑issuance.- Dormant‑account disablement reports.- Termination checklists with prompt access revocation.

Title: Password management policy enforced
Description: Strictly enforce the organization’s password management policy to guarantee compliance with security standards. Enforcing this policy includes implementing technical controls, monitoring adherence, and responding to non-compliance.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsDeploy automated mechanisms within authentication systems to enforce policy requirements, such as password complexity, length, and update frequency.Require MFA for access to sensitive systems. Integrate MFA settings into the authentication platform and verify regular usage, especially for high-risk and privileged accounts.Configure real-time alerts for repeated login failures or any detected password policy violations. Establish immediate corrective actions, such as account lockouts or password resets, to mitigate risks.Conduct periodic audits to verify policy enforcement across all systems and platforms. Address any gaps identified during audits, and document corrective actions taken to maintain compliance.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet monitors passing for supported integrations, with password policy enforcements configured across all critical applications.2. Screenshot of settings in your password manager (e.g., 1Password, Bitwarden, Azure AD) showing password complexity matches (or exceeds) the complexity defined in your Password Management Policy.3. Screenshots from all critical applications showing password complexity matches or exceeds the complexity defined in your Password Management Policy. Screenshots must display a visible system date/time.
Frameworks: cis_ig1_v1, gdpr_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800_171_v1, oneleet_essentials_v1, oneleet_pro_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1, soc2_v1

Title: Password management policy established
Description: Enforce a password management policy that mandates strong and complex passwords, and prohibits the reuse of previously used passwords. This policy helps protect user accounts from unauthorized access due to weak or compromised passwords.
Instructions: ### Action itemsCreate a password policy that requires complex passwords with a mix of characters, numbers, and symbols. This policy should also mandate regular password changes and prohibit the reuse of previous passwords.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Password policy uploaded to the Oneleet policy builder.2. PDF upload of the password policy with a dated version history.
Frameworks: cis_ig1_v1, gdpr_v1, hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, iso27001_v1, nist_800_171_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1, soc2_v1

Title: Secure authentication
Description: Secure authentication technologies and procedures shall be implemented based on information access restrictions and the topic-specific policy on access control.

Title: Signature during continuous session captured
Description: For a series of signings in a single, continuous, controlled session: require full credentials for the first signing; allow subsequent signings with at least one component (e.g., PIN) that only the individual can execute.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Require full authentication (ID + password + any required factor) at session start or first signing.2. Configure subsequent signings in that session to prompt for a signing‑PIN or re‑entry of password.3. Set inactivity timeout to force re‑authentication after idle period.4. Validate workflow to ensure at least one user‑only component is required for subsequent signings.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Configuration screenshots showing first‑sign and subsequent‑sign prompts.- Session timeout settings and tests.- Test records demonstrating first signing uses all components; later signings use PIN/password only.- SOP describing session rules.

Title: Signature outside continuous session captured
Description: When signings are not in a single continuous session, require all electronic signature components for each signing.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Enforce full authentication at each signing when no active controlled session exists.2. Disable any bypass/remember me for signing events initiated outside a session.3. Test that each such signing requires both identification components.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Test evidence showing signings outside a session require full ID + password (and factor if used).- Screenshots of settings disabling persistent sign‑on for signing events.- Audit logs showing full credential use per signing.

Title: Unique identification codes managed
Description: Manage IDs/passwords securely with lifecycle controls to prevent misuse.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Enforce periodic password changes.2. Disable credentials on role change/termination.3. Detect and report unauthorized attempts.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Password policy documentation.- Logs of password resets and expirations.- Audit of disabled accounts after user departure.

Title: Unique signature identification assigned
Description: Ensure each e‑signature is unique, non‑transferable, and tied to one individual.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Enforce unique credentials per user.2. Prohibit reuse or reassignment.3. Track signature ownership.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- System user registry showing unique ID per user.- Policy documenting no reuse.- Audit logs verifying unique signature usage.

Title: Unique user identification implemented
Description: Enforce the use of unique user identification for all individuals and systems accessing environments where protected information is stored, processed, or transmitted.
Instructions: Examples of evidence that fulfills this control:- A list of unique user IDs or account names for systems containing ePHI.- Screenshots or configurations showing enforcement of unique user authentication mechanisms.- Logs demonstrating access and activity tracking tied to unique identifiers.
Frameworks: hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1

Title: Use restricted to genuine owners
Description: Ensure electronic signatures (non‑biometric) are used only by their genuine owners.
Instructions: #### Action Items1. Prohibit credential sharing in policy and enforce via technical controls (unique IDs, monitoring for shared use).2. Enable anomaly detection for concurrent logins, impossible travel, or unusual IP/device use.3. Require MFA for signers and strong password policies.4. Investigate and remediate suspected misuse.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Policy excerpts prohibiting sharing; user acknowledgments.- SIEM/anomaly reports for concurrent or unusual use.- MFA enrollment reports and password policy settings.- Incident tickets for investigated misuse cases.

Title: AI data governance established
Description: Establish and maintain data governance processes for AI systems covering data acquisition, quality, provenance, preparation, and lifecycle management.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsDefine roles and responsibilities for data management, then implement quality metrics and validation processes to ensure data fitness for AI use. Track data sources and transformations to maintain lineage records throughout the AI lifecycle.Address privacy requirements, security controls, and bias detection mechanisms within your data governance framework.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Documented AI data governance framework and policies covering acquisition through disposal.2. Data quality requirements and metrics definitions with assessment reports.3. Data provenance tracking systems and records showing data origin and transformations.4. Data preparation standard operating procedures with version control logs.5. Privacy impact assessments for AI data processing and data access control policies.

Title: AI data lineage tracked
Description: Track and document data lineage throughout the AI system lifecycle, including data origin, transformations, usage, and flow from source to AI model outputs.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsImplement systems to record data sources, transformations, and processing steps for all AI datasets. Maintain metadata showing how data flows from original sources through preprocessing, training, and into production models.Create mapping records linking each model to its training data, enabling you to trace outputs back to source data and understand all applied transformations.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Data lineage tracking tool configurations (e.g., Apache Atlas, DataHub, Collibra) or manual tracking documentation.2. Data provenance records showing origin, transformations, and flow for AI datasets.3. Model-to-dataset mapping records linking models to their training data.4. Data flow diagrams showing data movement from sources through AI systems.

Title: AI data quality assessment performed
Description: Assess and ensure the quality of data used for AI systems meets defined requirements for accuracy, completeness, consistency, and fitness for purpose.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsSet quality thresholds for accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, and validity based on your AI system's objectives. Implement validation checks before using data for training or inference, using automated tools where possible.Document assessment results and track quality metrics over time to identify trends and remediate issues before they impact AI performance.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Data quality assessment reports showing results for accuracy, completeness, consistency, and other key metrics.2. Automated data validation and quality check configurations (e.g., Great Expectations, custom validation scripts).3. Documentation of quality issues identified and remediation actions taken.

Title: AI model validation performed
Description: Validate AI models to ensure they meet intended objectives, perform as expected in real-world conditions, and are fit for their intended purpose and use cases.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsTest models using data that reflects actual deployment conditions, measuring accuracy, precision, recall, and other relevant metrics. Assess performance across different scenarios and edge cases to confirm the model operates safely within defined boundaries.Include fairness assessments across demographic groups and conduct user acceptance testing before final approval.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. AI model validation plans and procedures documenting validation strategy and criteria.2. Performance validation test results showing accuracy, precision, recall, and other relevant metrics.3. Real-world validation reports from pilot deployments or A/B tests.

Title: AI system development process established
Description: Define and document processes for the responsible design, development, verification, validation, and deployment of AI systems throughout their lifecycle.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsDocument objectives and requirements for each AI system, then create design specifications and architecture diagrams. Integrate ethical and safety considerations into each phase of development, from initial design through final deployment.Establish clear acceptance criteria and verification methods, ensuring each AI system passes all required checks before deployment.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Documented AI system development lifecycle processes and methodology.2. AI system requirements specifications and traceability matrices linking requirements to implementation.3. Design documentation and architecture diagrams for AI systems.4. Verification and validation plans with test results and acceptance criteria.5. Deployment plans with pre-deployment checklists and approval records.

Title: AI system testing performed
Description: Perform comprehensive testing of AI systems to verify they meet specifications, function correctly, and are secure and reliable throughout their lifecycle.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsImplement unit tests, integration tests, and performance tests throughout the development lifecycle. Include security testing to identify vulnerabilities and adversarial attacks, plus regression testing to catch unintended changes.Automate tests in CI/CD pipelines where possible and maintain documentation showing test coverage and results.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Comprehensive test strategy and test plans covering all testing phases.2. Automated test suites and test automation framework configurations.3. Unit test coverage reports and integration test results.4. Performance test reports showing latency, throughput, and load testing metrics.5. Security test results including penetration test reports and adversarial testing results.

Title: Acceptable use of information and other associated assets
Description: Rules for the acceptable use and procedures for handling information and other associated assets shall be identified, documented and implemented.

Title: Automated Request Handling Workflow
Description: Establish an automated workflow for handling consumer privacy requests in compliance with the CCPA. The system captures, validates, and routes requests—such as access, deletion, or opt-out of sale—ensuring timely and accurate processing. Audit trails and reporting capabilities are maintained to demonstrate compliance and support ongoing monitoring and remediation.
Instructions: ### Action itemsImplement and configure an automated system to capture and validate all consumer privacy requests, including access, deletion, and opt-out of sale. Define workflows to route requests to the appropriate teams and ensure processing within CCPA-mandated timeframes. Maintain audit logs and generate periodic compliance reports to monitor performance and identify gaps for remediation.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. System-generated audit logs showing the receipt, validation, routing, and completion of consumer requests.2. Sample compliance reports demonstrating request processing metrics and timeliness.

Title: Classification of information
Description: Information shall be classified according to the information security needs of the organization based on confidentiality, integrity, availability and relevant interested party requirements.

Title: Clear Link to Not Share Information
Description: This control ensures that the website homepage includes clear and accessible links titled “Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information” and “Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information” in compliance with the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) and its amendments (CPRA). These links allow consumers to easily exercise their rights to opt out of the sale or sharing of their personal data and to restrict the use of sensitive personal information. The control verifies that the links are prominently displayed, functional, and direct users to appropriate mechanisms or preference centers for submitting their requests.
Instructions: ### Action itemsAdd clear links to "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" and "Limit use of my sensitive personal information"### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Screenshot of these buttons on the homepage.

Title: Collect Only Necessary Data
Description: Collect only the personal information necessary to fulfill its stated business purposes, in alignment with CCPA principles. Data minimization practices are applied across all collection points to limit exposure and reduce privacy risks. Regular reviews are conducted to ensure that unnecessary data is not captured or retained, supporting compliance and responsible data handling.
Instructions: ### Action itemsEstablish a process for ongoing reviews of data usage, and a process for limiting collection of data. Every noted type of data, in this document, should have a clear purpose. ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control* Process document for ongoing review of data usage, and data inventory.

Title: Compliance for multiple covered functions managed
Description: Entities performing multiple covered functions must establish clear processes to ensure each function adheres to its specific standards and requirements.
Instructions: Examples of evidence that fulfills this control- A list of covered functions with assigned roles and responsibilities.- Policies and procedures specific to each covered function.- Audit reports validating compliance with function-specific HIPAA standards.

Title: Conduct Privacy Impact Assessments for New Processes
Description: The organization must conduct privacy impact assessments (PIAs) for new processes being introduced to the data flow. Some example processes may be introducing a new plan for marketing emails, introducing a new payment processor, or introducing a new system for data analytics. This document should be approved by the privacy officer before action takes place.
Instructions: ### Action itemsAuthor a process document on how to complete a privacy assessment at your organization. This should include: a. Process for completing a threshold assessment (are we collecting, using, storing, or disclosing PI) b. Process for deciding how the PIA will be scoped, who will conduct it and write the report, budget, timeframe, and any other consultants c. Process for detailing the project d. Process for identifying stakeholders e. Process for mapping information flows f. Process for completing a privacy impact analysis and compliance checkg. Process for identifying areas to minimize or remove certain data types from the scope h. Process for finalizing recommendations based on the assessment i. Process for preparing the report, fielding responses, and any new further review.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. A process detailing when a PIA is completed, including details on how the process is completed.2. A completed assessment

Title: Confidential customer communication channel created
Description: Establish and maintain a secure communication channel for all customer interactions involving protected information.
Instructions: Examples of evidence that fulfills this control- Documentation of secure communication protocols for standard and alternative methods.- Records of system configurations or settings ensuring secure communications, such as encrypted email or secure messaging portals.- Staff training materials and attendance logs demonstrating knowledge of secure communication practices.

Title: Consent for collecting and managing data obtained
Description: Implement a consent mechanism that is freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. The consent requests must be clearly distinguishable from other matters, in an easily accessible form, using clear and simple language. For sensitive data, obtain explicit consent unless another exception applies.Maintain a record of obtained consents, including what the data subject has consented to and when.Regularly review and renew consents, especially if your purposes for processing change.Do not make services conditional on consent unless the data is necessary for that service.
Instructions: ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Documented consent forms and processes- Records of obtained consents- Screenshots of consent interfaces (e.g., web forms, mobile app screens)

Title: Cookie And Tracker Management
Description: This control requires the implementation of a Cookie and Tracker Management Platform (CMP) to manage user consent for cookies and tracking technologies in compliance with CCPA and related privacy regulations. The CMP collects, records, and enforces user preferences for data collection, sharing, and marketing purposes. It also provides mechanisms for users to review and update their consent, while maintaining audit logs to demonstrate regulatory compliance.
Instructions: ### Action itemsImplement a Cookie Tracker Management program that allows the user to choose which cookies they'd like to enable. The Cookie Manager should record and enforce user preferences for data collection and use, and maintains logs to prove that the cookie selections are honored### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Logs showing successful cookie opt-out, and a screenshot of the user interface for selection.

Title: Criminal record data processing managed
Description: If you process any data related to criminal convictions or offenses, verify that you have the legal authority to process this data.Implement strict access controls and security measures for this sensitive data. Document your legal basis and safeguards for processing criminal record data.Make sure you're not maintaining a comprehensive register of criminal convictions unless under official authority.
Instructions: ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- A documented legal analysis showing your authority to process criminal record data- Access logs demonstrating that only authorized personnel can view or modify criminal record data- A data processing agreement with a law enforcement agency, if applicable.- Internal policies and procedures detailing the safeguards in place for handling criminal record data

Title: Cryptographic key lifecycle managed
Description: Develop and document detailed key management policies and procedures covering the entire key lifecycle, including:- Implement secure key generation processes using strong cryptographic algorithms.- Establish secure methods for key distribution and storage.- Define and enforce key usage policies, including access controls and key rotation schedules.- Implement processes for secure key backup and recovery.- Establish procedures for key revocation, replacement, and destruction when necessary.- Implement split knowledge and dual control for manual cleartext key operations.- Prevent unauthorized substitution of cryptographic keys.- Require formal acknowledgment of responsibilities from key custodians.Regularly review and update key management practices to align with industry standards.
Instructions: ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Documented key management policies and procedures- Key generation and distribution logs- Key inventory and usage records- Key rotation and destruction logs- Signed acknowledgments from key custodians

Title: Cryptographic keys and application secrets secured
Description: Implement and maintain robust procedures to securely manage cryptographic keys and application secrets used for protecting sensitive data throughout their lifecycle.Develop and document a comprehensive key management policy and procedures, including:- Implement secure key generation processes using industry-standard algorithms and random number generators.- Establish secure key storage mechanisms, such as hardware security modules (HSMs) or encrypted key stores.- Enforce strict access controls to limit key/secret access to authorized personnel only.- Implement key rotation and expiration policies to minimize the impact of potential key compromises.- Set up secure key/secret distribution and transmission processes.- Establish and maintain an inventory of all cryptographic keys and their purposes.- Implement secure key/secret backup and recovery procedures.- Ensure proper destruction of retired or compromised keys and secrets.Regularly review and update key management practices to align with current industry standards and best practices.
Instructions: #### Evidence that fulfills this control:- Key management policy written in the Oneleet policy builder and signed by all employees.- Key inventory showing last rotation date.- Settings or screenshot of a key management SAAS tool like Doppler, AWS Secret Manager or Github Secrets.- Screenshot of settings that shows automated key rotation enabled on cloud infrastructure.
Frameworks: hipaa_business_associate_no_phi_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, nist_800_171_v1, pci_dss_level_1_v1

Title: Data deletion enforced
Description: Implement and maintain procedures to securely delete data when it is no longer needed, in compliance with data protection regulations and company policies.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsImplement data deletion procedures:- Use secure deletion methods (e.g., multi-pass overwriting for hard drives, crypto-shredding for cloud storage)- Apply deletion procedures to all relevant storage media (e.g., servers, workstations, mobile devices, backup systems)- Include both electronic and physical data (e.g., printed documents, USB drives)Automate deletion processes where possible by setting up automated deletion rules in data storage systems and using data lifecycle management tools if available.Conduct quarterly reviews of data storage systems to identify outdated data and verify that automated deletion processes are functioning correctly.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Data Retention Policy added to the Oneleet policy builder.2. Upload of your data retention policy showing retention periods and deletion methods.3. Dated logs of past data deletion activities4. Audit reports showing regular reviews of data deletion practices

Title: Data encrypted at rest
Description: All sensitive data is encrypted when stored on systems or devices.
Instructions: ### Action itemsEnsure all sensitive data stored on your systems or devices is encrypted. This can be done by using built-in encryption mechanisms offered by your operating systems and cloud providers or by leveraging third-party encryption tools.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Oneleet monitors passing for all supported integrations, with data encrypted on all in-scope systems where sensitive data is stored.2. Screenshots of encryption settings on all in-scope systems where sensitive data is stored.

Title: Data encrypted in-transit
Description: All data is encrypted when transmitted over networks, both within the organization's internal network and external connections.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsImplement protocols and tools to encrypt data in transit. Oneleet monitors passing for all supported integrations, with data encrypted in transit on all in-scope systems where sensitive data is transmitted.2. Title: Data inventory maintained
Description: Establish and maintain an accurate, detailed, and up-to-date inventory of all data assets. This can include data stored in databases, file shares, and cloud storage.

This includes both structured and unstructured data, and data stored on-premises as well as in the cloud.The inventory should provide details about each data asset, such as its type, location, owner, and classification in terms of sensitivity and business criticality. It should also be regularly reviewed and updated to reflect any changes in the organization's data landscape.Your data inventory should contain at least the following information:- Description- Location- Sensitivity level- Retention period- Backup frequency- Data owner### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Data inventory listing all data assets across your organization, including the date the inventory was last reviewed.

Title: Data labeled according to classification level
Description: All data is labeled based on its classification level. This ensures that appropriate access controls and handling procedures are applied to protect information.
Instructions: ### Action itemsImplement a data labeling system that is consistent and understood across the organization, ensuring that all employees are aware of the different classification ~~sensitivity~~ levels and the associated handling procedures.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- A screenshot of your data labeling procedures.- A screenshot showing your data labelling settings in relevant systems.

Title: Data leakage prevention
Description: Data leakage prevention measures shall be applied to systems, networks and any other devices that process, store or transmit sensitive information.

Title: Data management and retention policy established
Description: A data management and retention policy is established, outlining guidelines for how long data should be retained and how it should be managed throughout its lifecycle.
Instructions: ### Action itemsCreate and implement a data management and retention policy that is tailored to the specifics of your organization. This policy should detail what types of data are stored, how long they are retained, how they are managed during their lifecycle, and how they are securely disposed of when no longer needed.The policy must be communicated to all employees and they must acknowledge they have read and understood it.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Data management and retention policy uploaded to the Oneleet policy builder.2. PDF upload of the data management and retention policy with a dated version history.

Title: Data masking
Description: Data masking shall be used in accordance with the organization's topic-specific policy on access control and other related topic-specific policies, and business requirements, taking applicable legislation into consideration.

Title: Data masking procedures used
Description: Implement data masking procedures to replace sensitive or personally identifiable information with fictitious but realistic data. Ensure data masking is applied consistently across all non-production environments, such as development, testing, and training systems, to protect the confidentiality of sensitive data.
Instructions: ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Development overview of how data masking is used in test environments.2. Data inventory identifying which data is masked before use in non-production environments

Title: Data processing integrity and output validated
Description: Define and establish quality control measures to verify the completeness, accuracy, and timeliness of your output. This can look like implementing automated data validation checks, setting up data reconciliation processes, establishing review and approval workflows for critical outputs, creating data quality dashboards for real-time monitoring, and implementing version control systems for data processing scripts. Set up real-time monitoring of these measures and create error logs and incident reports where applicable.
Instructions: ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Documented quality control measures- Error logs and incident reports showing timely detection and resolution of processing errors- Audit trails of system processing activities, including timestamps and user actions
Frameworks: gdpr_v1, hipaa_business_associate_v1, hipaa_clearinghouse_v1, hipaa_health_plan_v1, hipaa_provider_v1, hipaa_v1, soc2_privacy_v1

Title: Data retention policy enforced
Description: The data retention policy is actively enforced, ensuring that data is retained for the appropriate duration and disposed of securely when it reaches the end of its retention period.
Instructions: ### Action itemsIf you do not have a data retention policy, it is important to develop one that clearly outlines how long each type of data should be kept, when it should be reviewed, and how it should be securely destroyed. Once the policy is in place, it should be communicated to all relevant staff and actively enforced.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Data Retention Policy added to the Oneleet policy builder.2. Oneleet monitors passing for supported integrations.3. A copy of your data retention policy document, showing your retention schedule.4. Evidence of data securely destroyed at the end of its retention period.

Title: Data transfer mechanisms established
Description: Establish proper data transfer mechanisms to protect personal data when it's transferred across borders or to third parties.Identify all instances where personal data is transferred outside your organization or to different jurisdictions. Assess the adequacy of data protection measures in recipient countries or organizations.Implement appropriate transfer mechanisms (e.g., standard contractual clauses, binding corporate rules) based on the nature of the transfer and the recipient.Establish processes to regularly review and update transfer mechanisms as regulatory requirements or organizational practices change. Maintain documentation of all data transfer arrangements and their legal bases.
Instructions: ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control- Documented inventory of all data transfers, including recipients and transfer mechanisms used- Signed agreements incorporating appropriate data transfer clauses (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses)- Records of adequacy assessments for recipient countries or organizations- Regular audit reports on the effectiveness and compliance of data transfer mechanisms

Title: De-identification process for PHI established
Description: Establish and implement a documented process for de-identifying protected health information (PHI) to comply with HIPAA requirements and enable the use or disclosure of data without patient authorization if required.
Instructions: Develop procedures to remove identifiers from PHI using HIPAA’s safe harbor method (removing all 18 direct identifiers) or expert determination method (certification by a qualified professional). Document the steps taken during the de-identification process, including verification of compliance with HIPAA standards. Maintain secure management of re-identification keys, ensuring they are stored separately and used only for approved purposes.Examples of evidence that fulfills this control- Written documentation of the de-identification process, specifying methods used (safe harbor or expert determination).- Logs of de-identified data sets created, including details of the identifiers removed.

Title: Establish Right-To-Correct Request Handling
Description: The organization must configure a system to handle Right-to-correct management. Under CCPA, the user has a right to have their inaccurate data corrected upon request.
Instructions: ### Action ItemsEstablish at least one accessible method for a user to express a right-to-correct request, such as: web form, email, or phone.### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. Screenshot of privacy policy section that includes methods of right-to-correct request handling, Screenshot of backend showing the process for handling. 2. Logs showing the process had been successfully completed for a current user.

Title: External privacy inquiries managed
Description: Establish and maintain a system for handling all privacy-related inquiries, including general questions, complaints, disputes, and specific requests for personal information access and correction.
Instructions: ### Examples of evidence that satisfy this control1. A screenshot or logs showing the date of the request, the type of request, resolution status, and the person who handled the inquiry.2. Documentation of authentication processes for verifying data subject identities when requesting access to their personal information.3. Samples of communication templates for various types of responses, including access grants, corrections, and denials with explanations.
